description: Design a cache invalidation strategy for a distributed ad serving platform with a read-your-writes consistency guarantee. Requirements: (1) 500K reads/sec and 10K writes/sec to user targeting profile data, (2) multiple geographically distributed nodes hold local in-process caches, (3) a read arriving at any node immediately after a confirmed write must never return stale data, (4) cache entries expire after 30 seconds. The system uses Redis as the shared cache layer with a pub/sub invalidation channel.
context: The read-your-writes guarantee must hold even when the cache invalidation message (published to Redis pub/sub after the database write commits) is delayed or reordered. Write path: application writes to PostgreSQL → commits → publishes invalidation to Redis pub/sub channel. Read path: application checks local in-process cache → if miss, reads from Redis → if miss, reads from PostgreSQL. The subtle failure mode to address: a write commits at PostgreSQL, the application publishes invalidation to Redis pub/sub, but a concurrent read at a different node executes between the PostgreSQL commit and the moment the invalidation message is delivered. That read will check local cache (hit — stale), return stale data, and violate read-your-writes. This window is typically 1–5ms but can be longer under network congestion.

checks.text: Does the proposal explicitly address the race window between the PostgreSQL commit and delivery of the Redis pub/sub invalidation message — not just describe cache invalidation in general?
Does the proposal provide a mechanism that guarantees a read arriving within the race window (1–5ms after write commits) returns fresh data, rather than relying solely on eventual consistency?
Does the proposal handle the case where the Redis pub/sub invalidation message is dropped entirely (e.g., subscriber disconnect) without leaving the cache permanently stale beyond the 30s TTL?

should_prune.description: Proposal that only describes publishing invalidation events to Redis pub/sub without addressing the delivery race window
should_prune.violates: TL/1 — must address the race window, not just describe the invalidation mechanism

should_prune.description: Proposal that relies on the 30s TTL as the sole correctness mechanism for read-your-writes
should_prune.violates: TL/2 — TTL alone means reads can return stale data for up to 30s after a write

should_pass: Proposal using write-through invalidation with a version token or fence token: writer stores a version stamp in Redis alongside invalidation; readers check version stamp before serving from local cache. Alternatively: writer temporarily sets a 'recently-written' flag in Redis for the affected key; readers detect the flag and bypass local cache to read from Redis directly within a short window (e.g. 5–10ms).
